Article 9(4) requires consideration of the effects and possible interaction from the combined application of the high-risk AI requirements in Chapter III, Section 2. | The SOP defines security controls such as SAST, SCA, DAST, encryption, audit logging, artifact signing, and penetration testing. It does **not** require targeted AI risk controls mapped to EU AI Act requirements such as data governance, human oversight, transparency, accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity, logging, and instructions for use as interacting controls. SOP §§4.1, 4.2, 5.2.3, 5.3, 5.4. |
| **7. Formal residual-risk acceptability criteria for AI hazards** | EU AI Act **Article 9(5)** requires the residual risk associated with each hazard, and the overall residual risk of the high-risk AI system, to be judged acceptable. | The SOP’s Threat Model Document includes inherent and residual risk, but it does **not** define AI hazard categories, residual-risk acceptability criteria, approval thresholds, or an overall AI-system residual-risk determination for health, safety, and fundamental-rights risks. SOP §5.2.2. |
| **8. Communication of residual AI risks to deployers/users** | EU AI Act **Article 9(5)(c)** requires provision of information under Article 13 and, where appropriate, training to deployers. Article 13(2)–(3) requires instructions for use with clear information on characteristics, capabilities, limitations, accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity, intended purpose, and foreseeable risk circumstances. | SOP-PENG-001 does not require an Article 13-style instructions-for-use package or deployer-facing disclosure of residual AI risks, limitations, intended purpose, performance boundaries, foreseeable misuse, or required operational controls. SOP training is internal engineering training, not deployer/user training. SOP §§9.1, 10.1. |
| **9. Consideration of deployer knowledge, training, and use environment** | EU AI Act **Article 9(5)** requires risk reduction to consider the technical knowledge, experience, education, training expected of the deployer, and the presumable context of use. | The SOP does not require design teams to document who the deployer is, what training or competence they are expected to have, the clinical/operational environment of use, or how those assumptions affect risk controls and instructions. SOP §§5.1, 5.2. |
| **10. AI-specific testing tied to risk-management measures** | EU AI Act **Article 9(6)** requires high-risk AI systems to be tested to identify the most appropriate and targeted risk-management measures and to ensure consistent performance for the intended purpose and compliance with high-risk requirements. | The SOP requires security testing, DAST, penetration testing, SAST/SCA, and QA abuse testing. It does **not** require AI risk-control validation such as clinical performance validation, subgroup performance testing, bias/fairness testing, robustness testing, drift testing, human-oversight effectiveness testing, fail-safe behavior testing, or validation that risk controls reduce health, safety, and fundamental-rights risks. SOP §§5.3.3, 5.4. |
| **11. Predefined AI metrics and probabilistic thresholds** | EU AI Act **Article 9(8)** requires testing to be carried out against **prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds** appropriate to the intended purpose. | The SOP defines security pass/fail thresholds, such as zero Critical/High SAST findings and zero Critical/High DAST findings, but it does **not** require predefined AI performance thresholds such as sensitivity, specificity, false positive/negative limits, calibration, subgroup thresholds, confidence thresholds, uncertainty thresholds, robustness thresholds, or safety-performance thresholds. SOP §§5.3.3, 5.4.2, 7.2. |
| **12. Testing throughout development and before placing on market or putting into service** | EU AI Act **Article 9(8)** requires testing, as appropriate, throughout development and in any event before the system is placed on the market or put into service. | The SOP has development and release gates, but it does **not** specifically require high-risk AI Article 9 testing before market placement or putting into service, nor does it define AI validation checkpoints across model development, data updates, retraining, or material model changes. SOP §§5.3–5.5. |
| **13. Children and vulnerable-group impact assessment** | EU AI Act **Article 9(9)** requires providers to consider whether the high-risk AI system is likely to adversely impact persons under 18 and, as appropriate, other vulnerable groups. | The SOP does not require a specific assessment of impacts on minors, patients, elderly persons, persons with disabilities, economically vulnerable groups, or other vulnerable populations. This is especially relevant for health AI, but the SOP only addresses PHI/PII and security/privacy controls. SOP §§1.2, 4.2, 5.1. |
| **14. Traceable AI risk-management file** | EU AI Act **Article 9(1)** requires the risk management system to be documented and maintained. Article 9(2) requires the system to cover identification, evaluation, post-market risk review, and measures. | The SOP requires a Threat Model Document and design checklist, but it does **not** require a maintained AI risk-management file with traceability from intended purpose → hazards → affected rights/safety interests → risk estimation → controls → verification/validation evidence → residual-risk acceptance → deployer information → post-market findings. SOP §§5.2.2, 5.2.3, 5.5.1. |
| **15. Integration with Article 72 post-market monitoring and serious AI risk escalation** | EU AI Act **Article 9(2)(c)** depends on Article 72 post-market monitoring data. Article 72(1)–(2) requires active and systematic collection, documentation, and analysis of performance data throughout the AI system lifetime. | The SOP’s escalation and vulnerability-management process is security-centered. It does not define triggers for AI performance degradation, emerging bias, harmful recommendations, serious AI incidents, deployer complaints, model drift, or market feedback to re-open the Article 9 risk assessment. SOP §§5.6, 8.3. |

## Bottom line

SOP-PENG-001 is a strong **secure SDLC** procedure, but it is not yet a complete **EU AI Act Article 9 risk-management system** for high-risk AI. The largest gaps are that it treats AI/ML mainly as software/security artifacts, while Article 9 requires a documented, lifecycle-wide, AI-specific risk process covering **health, safety, fundamental rights, foreseeable misuse, post-market performance data, residual-risk acceptability, deployer information/training, vulnerable groups, and AI-specific validation metrics/thresholds**.

Compliance level: partial
